国史 (Kokushi) is from the title, (見在) present) 社 shrine
It literally means shrine present in the [[Rikkokushi]]
== References ==

revision.comment: added [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
[[Category:Kokushi Genzaisha]] [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]] Kokushi Genzaisha (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It refers to shrines which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
国史 (Kokushi) is from the title, (見在) present) 社 shrine
It literally means shrine present in the [[Rikkokushi]]
Shrines that appear in both are called [[Myojin Taisha]]
== References ==

[[Category:Kokushi Genzaisha]] [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]] Kokushi Genzaisha (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It refers to shrines which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[Nihon kōki]], Shoku nihon kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine
== References ==

[[Category:Kokushi Genzaisha]] [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]] Kokushi Genzaisha (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It refers to shrines which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[Nihon kōki]], Shoku nihon kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine
== References == [[ja:国史見在社]]

revision.text:
'''Kokushi genzaisha''' (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It means a shrine that appears in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine
== References == {{reflist}}
{{shinto shrines}}

The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine

'''Kokushi genzaisha''' (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It means a shrine that appears in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine
== References == {{reflist}}
{{shinto shrines}}

Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[Myojin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine

Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine

The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[:en:Nihon kōki|Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Articles needing translation from Japanese Wikipedia]] to [[Category:need translation]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kokushi genzaisha]][[fr:Kokushi genzaisha]][[ja:国史見在社]][[simple:Kokushi genzaisha]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kokushi genzaisha]][[fr:Kokushi genzaisha]][[ja:国史見在社]][[simple:Kokushi genzaisha]] {{Expand Japanese|国史見在社|date=November 2025}} <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kokushi genzaisha]][[fr:Kokushi genzaisha]][[ja:国史見在社]][[simple:Kokushi genzaisha]]
'''Kokushi genzaisha''' (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It means a shrine that appears in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[:en:Nihon kōki|Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine

'''Kokushi genzaisha''' (国史見在社) are a type of Shinto shrine. It means a shrine that appears in the [[Rikkokushi]] (六国史) but not in the Engishiki<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The [[Rikkokushi]] or the Six Official Histories, includes [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon shoki]], [[Shoku Nihongi|Shoku nihongi]], [[:en:Nihon kōki|Nihon kōki]], Shoku Nihon Kōki, Montoku jitsuroku, and Sandai jitsuroku. Kokushi gensaisha are also called kokushi shozaisha or "shrines that appear in the Official Histories". These shrines are important because they have a historical significance and are regarded as ancient shrines. Some of the shrines listed in the Jinmyōchō (Register of Deities) in Engishiki, also known as [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]] also overlap with the kokushi genzaisha, but the term usually refers to shrines that are only mentioned in the Official Histories.<ref name=":0" />
国史 (Kokushi) means official history, 見在 gensai means appearing and 社 sha means shrine
